company_profile.name: Graham Holdings Company
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Graham Holdings Company, through its subsidiaries, operates as a diversified holding company in the United States and internationally. The company provides academic preparation programs for international students; professional training and postsecondary education services, as well as English-language programs; operations support services for pre-college, certificate, undergraduate and graduate programs; exam preparation services; career and academic advisement services; and operates a sixth-form college that prepares students for A-level examinations. It also owns and operates television broadcast stations, restaurants, and entertainment venues; and offers social media management tools to connect newsrooms with their users. In addition, the company offers in-home specialty pharmacy infusion therapies; home health, hospice and palliative services; applied behavior analysis therapy; physician services for allergy, asthma and immunology patients; in-home aesthetics; and healthcare software-as-a-service technology. Further, it operates as a multi-product supplier to the commercial building industry; manufactures electrical and lifting solutions; and supplies parts used in electric utilities and industrial systems. Additionally, the company operates dealerships and valet repair services; provides custom framing services; marketing solutions; customer data and analytics software; Slate and Foreign Policy magazines; daily local news podcast and newsletter; a software-as-a-service platform that monetize audio content through paid subscriptions, memberships, and audiobooks; operates an online art gallery and in-person art fair business; and an online commerce platform that features original art and designs on an array of consumer products. The company was formerly known as The Washington Post Company and changed its name to Graham Holdings Company in November 2013. Graham Holdings Company was founded in 1877 and is based in Arlington, Virginia.
company_profile.description_summary:
# Graham Holdings Company

A diversified holding company generating revenue through education services, including academic preparation and professional training programs; television broadcasting; healthcare services encompassing specialty pharmacy and home health; industrial manufacturing and supply; automotive dealerships; and media properties including Slate and Foreign Policy magazines. Additionally operates restaurants, entertainment venues, and digital platforms for content monetization and e-commerce.

The company's distinctive portfolio reflects its evolution from a newspaper publisher, now spanning healthcare delivery, educational technology, and creative ventures while maintaining significant media operations through digital and print publications.

_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

_meta.type_rationale: Asset manager/broker — DNI prioritised; standard CF metrics unreliable
_meta.method_explanation: Asset managers and brokers earn fees for managing other people's money. Their balance sheets hold client assets that do not belong to the firm, which makes standard balance sheet metrics like Price-to-Book misleading. Instead, we focus on net income and revenue-based methods, which better reflect the underlying fee-earning power of the business.

company_profile.description: Global Industrial Company, through its subsidiaries, operates as an industrial distributor of various industrial and maintenance, repair, and operation (MRO) products in the United States and Canada. company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Globant S.A., together with its subsidiaries, provides technology services in the United States, rest of North America, Latin America, Europe, and internationally. The company offers Digital Studio, which integrates artificial intelligence into the software development; GUT Studio, which allows clients to better connect brands; and Enterprise Studio, which leverages technology for streamlined operations and productivity. It also provides AI Industry Studios for the financial services; media, entertainment, sports, and leisure; healthcare and life sciences; CPG, retail, and automotive; gaming and EdTech; airlines; energy, oil, and gas; and high tech and professional services sectors. In addition, the company offers AI Pods, a subscription-based delivery model for AI-powered services; Globant Enterprise AI, an agentic innovation platform; Corporate Hub, which grounds intelligence in how the organization operates; AI Hub, which connects and governs foundation and custom models, continuous evaluation, and optimization; and Agents Hub, which enables autonomous action through the creation and coordination of agents and agentic workflows. Further, it provides GeneXus for enterprise systems evolution; Globant CODA for software development; Navigate Digital Twin for process optimization; Navigate Service Assist for support; and Fusion, for marketing, communications, and advertising, as well as StarMeUp, an employee engagement and talent experience platform; and DaXia, an embedded finance accelerator platform. Globant S.A. has a strategic collaboration with Pharma Mar, S.A. for cancer drug discovery through artificial intelligence; and Viability and Cascadience LLC to launch AI-powered platform for students with disabilities. The company was formerly known as IT Outsourcing S.L. and changed its name to Globant S.A. in December 2012. Globant S.A. was founded in 2003 and is based in Luxembourg, Luxembourg.
company_profile.description_summary:
Globant provides technology services globally, specializing in artificial intelligence-integrated software development, brand connectivity solutions, and enterprise operations optimization. The company differentiates itself through proprietary AI platforms including subscription-based AI Pods, an agentic innovation platform, and specialized tools for digital transformation, process optimization, and employee engagement across industries.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: General Mills benefits from strong brand recognition and pricing power in packaged foods—evidenced by a 12% profit margin and 23.6% return on equity—but a weak 10.4% return on invested capital reveals the moat is moderate rather than fortress-like, as the company struggles to deploy capital efficiently despite its market position.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 12% profit margin and 23.6% return on equity both sit comfortably above what typical consumer packaged goods companies achieve, signaling General Mills converts sales into profit effectively and generates solid returns for shareholders. The company's brand portfolio—including Cheerios, Betty Crocker, and Yoplait—gives it pricing power that most food manufacturers lack. However, the 10.4% return on invested capital lags well behind the 15–20% range expected of truly excellent businesses, indicating that despite high profitability, the company isn't deploying its total assets as efficiently as market leaders do.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 0.67 sits below the healthy 1.0–1.5 threshold, meaning General Mills has less than $0.67 in liquid assets for every dollar of near-term obligations—a yellow flag for financial flexibility. The zero debt-to-EBITDA ratio is exceptional and removes leverage risk from the equation entirely, showing the company has paid down debt aggressively. Yet the tight liquidity position caps the overall score at adequate rather than strong, leaving little room for unexpected downturns or opportunistic investments without raising new capital.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 2.2% per year is sluggish—roughly half the long-term inflation rate—and reflects a mature, slow-moving industry where General Mills is unlikely to expand its market share meaningfully. Worse, earnings per share is projected to decline 7.2% annually over the next five years, meaning shareholders will see their ownership stake in future profits shrink even if the business stays stable. The company explicitly notes that growth is constrained, confirming management's own view that the packaged foods business is unlikely to be a driver of returns.
metric_assessments.predictability: Steady margins and a clear, repetitive customer base (breakfast cereals and baking products are staples with predictable demand) give General Mills above-average visibility into future earnings relative to volatile industries. The 6/10 score reflects a business where you can reasonably forecast revenues and profits over a 2–3 year horizon. However, the company is exposed to commodity costs (grains, dairy) and changing consumer preferences toward fresher, less processed foods—two headwinds that introduce some unpredictability and prevent a higher score.
metric_assessments.valuation: An 8/10 valuation score indicates the stock is trading at a reasonable or slightly attractive price relative to its earnings and assets, offering decent value for the risk. With slowing growth and earnings headwinds ahead, the market has already priced in low expectations, which protects downside but also limits upside surprise potential. The low price is fair compensation for a mature business, though it doesn't offer a significant margin of safety against disappointment.

The company is not at risk of a crisis, but it has less financial flexibility than a pristine operator would.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 20% and expected earnings growth of 30.5% over five years signal strong momentum and suggest the market is hungry for Corning's products. These rates are well above the 8–12% typical of mature industrial companies, and they indicate the business is capturing share or entering new markets. The offsetting concern is that this growth is flagged as constrained, implying there are structural or competitive limits ahead that will eventually slow expansion.
metric_assessments.predictability: With a predictability score of 4/10, earnings are volatile and hard to forecast quarter to quarter. Corning's results depend heavily on customer demand cycles, raw material costs, and capital spending trends in telecom and consumer electronics—all notoriously lumpy sectors. This unpredictability makes it harder for investors to have confidence in long-term returns and increases the risk that guidance misses or surprise downturns catch shareholders off guard.

company_profile.description_summary:

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (industrials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.name: Gold.com, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Asset manager/broker — DNI prioritised; standard CF metrics unreliable
company_profile.description: Gold.com, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, operates as a precious metals company. It operates through three segments: Wholesale Sales & Ancillary Services, Direct-to-Consumer, and Secured Lending. The Wholesale Sales & Ancillary Services segment sells gold, silver, platinum, and palladium in the form of bars, plates, powders, wafers, grains, ingots, and coins. This segment also offers complementary services, such as receiving, handling, inventorying, processing, packing, and shipping of precious metals and custom coins on a secure basis; and designs and produces minted silver products. The Direct-to-Consumer segment provides access to gold, silver, copper, platinum, and palladium products primarily through its websites; rarities and numismatic collections; and numismatic and bullion products. It operates various websites targeting specific niches within the precious metals retail market. This segment also operates as a direct retailer of precious metals to the investor community and markets its precious metal products on television, radio, and the internet, as well as through customer service outreach. The Secured Lending segment originates and acquires commercial loans secured by bullion, numismatic coins, and graded sports cards. The company serves customers, including financial institutions, bullion retailers, industrial manufacturers and fabricators, sovereign mints, refiners, coin and metal dealers, investors, collectors, and e-commerce and other retail customers. It operates in the United States, Europe, Canada, Asia Pacific, Africa, Australia, and South America. The company was formerly known as A-Mark Precious Metals, Inc. and changed its name to Gold.com, Inc. in December 2025. The company was founded in 1965 and is headquartered in Costa Mesa, California.
company_profile.description_summary:
This precious metals company operates through three main segments: wholesale sales of gold, silver, platinum, and palladium in various forms; direct-to-consumer retail through multiple websites targeting niche markets; and secured lending backed by bullion and collectibles. It serves financial institutions, manufacturers, retailers, investors, and collectors globally.

The company's distinctive strengths include a diversified product range spanning bullion and numismatic items, multi-channel marketing via television and digital platforms, and ancillary services like secure handling and custom coin production.

_meta.type_rationale: Speculative growth — EPS fwd 29.2%, profitability 3/10; P/S and PSG prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: High-growth companies — typically in technology or biotech — are often not yet profitable, which means earnings-based methods like P/E give no useful result. Instead, we use Price-to-Sales (P/S), which compares the stock price to total revenue regardless of whether the company is making a profit. The PSG ratio (Price/Sales/Growth) also adjusts for how fast revenue is growing. These methods are imperfect but they are the best available tools when a company is still investing heavily to grow.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Generac has moderate competitive advantages anchored in distribution and brand recognition, but thin profit margins and low returns on capital suggest limited pricing power or cost leadership.

metric_assessments.profitability: At 4.4%, Generac's profit margin sits well below the 8–12% typical for healthy industrial businesses, indicating the company struggles to convert revenue into earnings. The company is generating sales growth but surrendering most of that to costs, which suggests either intense competition, commodity-like pricing, or operational inefficiency. This weakness directly caps the moat, since a truly defensible business normally earns fatter margins by leveraging its advantages.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 2.03 shows solid short-term liquidity, and the balance sheet has room to breathe compared to many peers. However, a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.64x is moderately elevated—not dangerous, but it means the company is carrying meaningful financial obligations that constrain flexibility. For a speculative growth company, this represents decent stability, though it leaves limited cushion if cash flows disappoint.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue is growing at 12.4% annually, a respectable pace, but the company expects earnings per share to grow only 29.2% over five years—roughly 5.2% annually when annualized—which falls short of typical growth-stock expectations. Growth appears constrained by the business model itself (evidenced by the constraint flag), meaning Generac faces structural headwinds that prevent it from scaling profitably at faster rates. This slowdown is the core reason the growth score remains weak despite solid top-line momentum.
metric_assessments.predictability: With a 7/10 score, Generac's earnings are relatively stable and recurring, likely because backup power systems and home standby generators serve steady demand across economic cycles. The company has shown it can forecast revenues and margins consistently, which gives investors confidence in guidance. That said, the moderate score reflects exposure to housing cycles and potential disruption from solar+battery alternatives, which introduce some long-term uncertainty.
metric_assessments.valuation: A 0/10 valuation score signals the stock is significantly overpriced relative to fundamentals—the market is pricing in far more growth or profitability than the business currently delivers. With weak profitability (4.4% margin), weak returns on capital (5.71% ROIC, well below the 12–15% cost of capital), and constrained growth, there is little earnings power to justify a premium multiple. An investor buying today is betting the company will dramatically improve operations or capture new markets, not simply buying a fairly priced, steady business.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Generac is rated Dangerous because it combines weak profitability and anemic returns on capital with expensive valuation, creating a risky mismatch. The business is not broken—it has decent liquidity and predictable revenue—but it is not generating enough cash profit relative to its valuation to offer a margin of safety. An investor overpaying for a slow-growing, low-margin business faces the risk that growth disappoints, valuation contracts, and the stock falls sharply.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Generac is a established industrial company with sticky, recurring revenue from backup power systems, but it suffers from thin 4.4% profit margins and returns on capital of only 5.71%—both significantly below what investors expect from quality businesses. The company is growing revenue at 12.4%, yet earnings growth is constrained to around 5% annually, indicating the business hits structural limits in converting additional sales into profits. Financial stability is reasonable with a 2.03 current ratio, though moderate leverage at 2.64x debt-to-EBITDA leaves limited room for setbacks. Most critically, the stock is priced for perfection—a 0/10 valuation score—meaning the market has already priced in best-case scenarios with no margin of error. For most beginner investors, this is a setup to avoid: you would be overpaying for a business that is neither growing fast enough nor profitable enough to justify the price, making it a speculative bet rather than a sound long-term holding.

_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.description: Graphic Packaging Holding Company, together with its subsidiaries, engages in the design, production, and sale of consumer packaging products to brands in food, beverage, foodservice, household, and other consumer products in the Americas, Europe, and the Asia Pacific. It operates through two segments, Americas Paperboard Packaging and International Paperboard Packaging. The Americas Paperboard Packaging segment includes paperboard packaging sold primarily to consumer packaged goods (CPG) companies serving the food, beverage and consumer product markets and cups, lids and food containers sold primarily to foodservice companies and quick-service restaurants in the Americas. The International Paperboard Packaging includes paperboard packaging sold primarily to CPG companies serving the food, foodservice, beverage and consumer product markets, including healthcare and beauty, outside of the Americas. The company also designs and manufactures specialized packaging machinery, differentiating it from pure packaging producers.

company_profile.name: Genuine Parts Company
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Genuine Parts Company distributes automotive and industrial replacement parts. The company operates in three segments: North America Automotive Parts Group, International Automotive Parts Group, and Industrial Parts Group. It distributes automotive replacement parts, accessories, tools, equipment, and related solutions for hybrid and electric vehicles, trucks, buses, motorcycles, farm equipment, and heavy-duty equipment. The company also offers replacement parts, including brakes, batteries, filters, engine components, and fluids; specialized services, such as paint mixing, hydraulic hose assembly, battery testing, and key cutting; and accessories and specialty equipment for automotive and heavy-duty vehicles, as well as tools and diagnostic devices for repair and maintenance. In addition, it provides independent repair shops and auto care centers under the NAPA brand, and offers technical expertise and training programs to customers. Further, the company provides bearings, seals, and gaskets; hose, fittings, hydraulics, and pneumatics components; abrasives, adhesives, sealants, and tape; pumps and power transmission; tools and testing equipment; electrical supplies and safety products; and chemicals and janitorial supplies. Additionally, it offers inventory management; vendor-managed inventory; asset repair and tracking, including radio frequency identification; and specialized repair services for gearboxes, fluid power systems, pumps, drive shafts, electrical panels, and hoses and gaskets. The company was incorporated in 1928 and is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia.
company_profile.description_summary:
This distributor supplies automotive and industrial replacement parts across three segments: North America and International Automotive, plus Industrial Parts. It distributes components like brakes, batteries, and filters; offers specialized services including paint mixing and hydraulic assembly; and provides technical expertise and training through its NAPA brand network.

The company distinguishes itself through comprehensive inventory management solutions, including vendor-managed inventory and asset tracking technology, alongside specialized repair services for complex systems like gearboxes and fluid power components across automotive and industrial sectors.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: GPC's weak profitability and razor-thin margins leave almost no room for competitive advantage; even its predictable earnings cannot overcome the reality that the business generates minimal returns on capital relative to what strong competitors achieve.

metric_assessments.profitability: At 0.2% profit margin, GPC is keeping roughly 20 cents of profit for every $10,000 in sales—far below the 5-10% margins that even average industrial and distribution businesses typically post. The company is managing to operate without major losses, which shows basic operational competence, but the margin is so thin that any cost increase or competitive pressure could squeeze profitability to nothing. This lack of pricing power or cost control is the core reason profitability scored only 3/10.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 1.08 means GPC has just $1.08 in short-term assets for every $1 in short-term obligations—barely above the minimum safety threshold of 1.0 and below the 1.5 ratio that signals comfortable liquidity. The debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.3x is moderate for an industrial distributor but still means the company would need more than three years of profit to pay down its debt, which limits financial flexibility during downturns. The company is solvent and not in crisis, but it has little cushion to weather disruptions or invest in the business.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 6.8% is reasonable for a mature distributor but constrained by the company's own operational limits—the data explicitly flags that growth is being held back by structural factors. Projected EPS growth of 7.9% over five years is marginally ahead of revenue growth, suggesting modest improvements in efficiency, yet this still lags the 10-12% long-term growth that stronger companies in the sector achieve. The business is expanding, but at a pedestrian pace that limits upside for shareholders.
metric_assessments.predictability: Earnings have proven stable and forecastable enough to justify a 7/10 score—GPC's business model as a parts distributor generates recurring revenue from maintenance and repair needs that don't vanish in downturns. However, this predictability sits atop a cyclical sector (Consumer Cyclical) where demand still fluctuates with economic conditions, and the thin margins mean earnings surprises can swing sharply if input costs or customer mix shift even slightly. The business is predictable in direction, but not immune to meaningful swings in absolute profit.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 6/10, the stock appears fairly priced relative to its earnings power and growth rate, neither a bargain nor overpriced. The company's weak profitability and constrained growth mean there is less margin of safety than you'd get with higher-quality distributors trading at similar multiples, so fair value doesn't feel particularly attractive when the underlying business quality is below-average.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: GPC is rated Dangerous because its profit margins are dangerously thin (0.2%), leaving almost no room for error or downturns, and its returns on invested capital (8.72%) are below what you'd expect from a strong business. Combined with moderate debt levels (3.3x debt-to-EBITDA) and tight working capital (1.08 current ratio), the company has limited financial flexibility if economic conditions weaken or competition intensifies. For a beginner, this means the business is stable but fragile—it works fine in normal times, but stress could quickly become severe.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Genuine Parts is a stable, predictable distributor of automotive and industrial parts with reliable recurring revenue, but it earns almost nothing per dollar of sales, making it a low-return business even when things go well. The company's debt is manageable and growth is steady at nearly 7%, yet the combination of razor-thin margins and constrained growth means shareholders are unlikely to see meaningful wealth creation over the next five years. The stock trades at a fair price, but fair value for a below-average business is not the same as a good investment; you're paying a reasonable price for something that doesn't deliver strong returns. A beginner investor would be better served buying higher-quality distributors or diversified companies with better margins and growth. Pass on this one.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.description: The Gorman-Rupp Company designs, manufactures, and sells pumps and pump systems in the United States and internationally. Its products are used in water, wastewater, construction, dewatering, industrial, petroleum, chemical processing, original equipment, agriculture, fire suppression, heating, ventilating and air conditioning, military, and other liquid-handling applications. The company markets its products through a network of distributors, manufacturers' representatives, third-party distributor catalogs, direct sales, retailers, and e-commerce. The Gorman-Rupp Company was founded in 1933 and is headquartered in Mansfield, Ohio.
company_profile.description_summary: This company designs, manufactures, and sells a diverse range of pumps and pump systems globally. Its offerings span self-priming centrifugal, submersible, rotary gear, diaphragm, and numerous other pump types serving water treatment, industrial, petroleum, agricultural, and fire suppression applications. Distribution occurs through distributors, direct sales, retailers, and e-commerce channels.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.description: Global Payments Inc. provides payment technology and software solutions for card, check, and digital-based payments in the Americas, Europe, and the Asia-Pacific. Beyond core payment processing, the company differentiates itself through comprehensive vertical-market software solutions that streamline broader business operations, reaching customers via direct sales, resellers, and financial institution partnerships.

_meta.type_rationale: Asset manager/broker — DNI prioritised; standard CF metrics unreliable
